coredata.dc:title: A versatile cellulose nanocrystal‑carbon dots architecture: Preparation and environmental/biological applications
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Ruyi
Liang, Fangyuan
Hu, Xianyun
Bian, Huiyang
Deng, Chao
Seidi, Farzad
Zhang, Baiyang
Xiao, Huining
Liu, Yuqian
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a versatile cellulose nanocrystal‑carbon dots (CNC-CDs) architecture is prepared and applied in environmental remediation and biological sensing. The citric acid (CA) is employed to extract CNCs and in-situ synthesize blue-emissive CDs. The carboxyl-rich surface of CNC-CDs enables the chelation of Hg2+ and fluorescence quenching of CDs, realizing the detection and adsorption of Hg2+ with a film comprised of the architecture. The limit of detection (LOD) is measured as 12.8 nM with satisfying retention efficiency. The versatility of CNC-CDs is demonstrated by the fabrication of a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) sensor using m-tetrakis(4-sulfonatophenyl)porphine (TSPP) as FRET acceptor and Fe3+ receptor. Fluorescence of the sensor is responsive to Fe3+ in a ratiometric manner, which significantly contributes to the visualized and sensitive Fe3+ detection (6.9 nM). In combination with excellent biocompatibility, the FRET sensor is capable of imaging intracellular Fe3+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose nanocrystal
Carbon dot
Ferric ion
Fluorescence
Mercury ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hg2+ detection and removal with CNC-CDs film
Fe3+ detection in solution and intracellular Fe3+ imaging
Construction of CNC-CDs architecture via successive hydrolysis‑carbonization
Facile fabrication of the CNC-CDs film
Sensitive detection and efficient removal of Hg2+
Design and construction of the CNC-CDs based FRET sensor
Sensing of Fe3+ by tuning the FRET efficiency and fluorescence ratio of the sensor
Visualization and semi-quantification of intracellular Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the successive hydrolysis‑carbonization strategy for preparation of CNC-CDs architecture.

(A) FT-IR spectra of CNCs, CNC-CA and CNC-CDs. (B) TEM images of CNC-CA and CNC-CDs. (C) Zeta potentials, and (D) XRD patterns of CNCs, CNC-CA and CNC-CDs. (E) Fluorescence spectra of CNC-CA and CNC-CDs. (F) Absorption spectra of CNC-CA, CNC-CDs and CDs.

(A) Illustration of the film fabrication by the suction filtration, and the digital photographs of CNC-CDs film taken under ambient light and UV irradiation. (B) SEM images of a) CNC-CA film and b) CNC-CDs film.

(A) Illustrations of the Hg2+ detection and removal with the CNC-CDs film and the proposed PET mechanism for the fluorescence quenching of CDs. (B) Evolution of the relative fluorescence intensity of the film versus Hg2+ concentration. The inset shows the fluorescent images of the films treated with different amounts of Hg2+. (C) Relative fluorescence intensity of the films treated with varying metal ions. (C) Concentrations of Hg2+ before ( C
0) and after ( C
e) filtration through the film, and the corresponding removal efficiency. (D) Hg2+ removal efficiency, as well as the fluorescent images of the CNC-CDs film in four rounds of recycling.

(A) Schematic of construction of the FRET sensor. Zeta potentials of (B) CNC-CDs upon coating with PEI and (C) CNC-CDs@PEI upon loading TSPP. (D) Evolution of fluorescence spectra of the CNC-CDs@PEI@TSPP with varying TSPP densities. (E) Fluorescence intensity ratio of the CNC-CDs@PEI@TSPP as a function of TSPP amount. I
B/ I
R represents the blue-to-red fluorescence intensity ratio, respectively.

(A) Illustration of Fe3+ detection with the sensor relying on the suppressed FRET from CDs to TSPP. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs and absorption spectra of TSPP with varying Fe3+ concentrations. (C) Fluorescence spectra and images of the FRET sensor treated with varying amounts of Fe3+. (D) Evolution of relative fluorescence ratio as a function of the Fe3+ concentration. (E) Blue and red fluorescence intensities, and the relative fluorescence ratio of the sensor toward different metal ions. For simulating the physiological condition, concentrations are 140 mM for Na+, 5.0 mM for K+, 2.5 mM for Ca2+, 1.0 mM for Mg2+, 20 μM for Zn2+ and Fe2+, 1.0 μM for Cr3+, and 0.5 μM for Ni2+, Co2+, and Hg2+.

Fluorescent images of HeLa cells treated with the FRET sensor and 0–800 nM of Fe3+. Scale bar: 20 μm. Histograms show intracellular fluorescent intensities collected at the blue channel (B-ch) and red channel (R-ch).
Chemical analysis for hydroxyl-terminated CNCs, CNC-CA, and CNC-CDs.

head.ce:title: A versatile cellulose nanocrystal‑carbon dots architecture: Preparation and environmental/biological applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a versatile cellulose nanocrystal‑carbon dots (CNC-CDs) architecture is prepared and applied in environmental remediation and biological sensing. The citric acid (CA) is employed to extract CNCs and in-situ synthesize blue-emissive CDs. The carboxyl-rich surface of CNC-CDs enables the chelation of Hg2+ and fluorescence quenching of CDs, realizing the detection and adsorption of Hg2+ with a film comprised of the architecture. The limit of detection (LOD) is measured as 12.8 nM with satisfying retention efficiency. The versatility of CNC-CDs is demonstrated by the fabrication of a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) sensor using m-tetrakis(4-sulfonatophenyl)porphine (TSPP) as FRET acceptor and Fe3+ receptor. Fluorescence of the sensor is responsive to Fe3+ in a ratiometric manner, which significantly contributes to the visualized and sensitive Fe3+ detection (6.9 nM). In combination with excellent biocompatibility, the FRET sensor is capable of imaging intracellular Fe3+.

Fluorescent CNC-CDs are fabricated by successive esterification‑carbonization, and shows excellent versatility in environmental and biomedical applications.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) are a class of nanomaterials prepared by the “top-down” degradations of plentiful biomass resources (Vanderfleet & Cranston, 2020; Xie et al., 2019). The as-prepared CNCs could be capped by diverse kinds of groups, allowing flexible modifications (Cheng et al., 2020; Li, Jin, et al., 2022; Li, Liu, et al., 2022). Among which, fluorescently-functionalized CNCs (F-CNCs) can provide the unique optical properties of emissive moieties, and simultaneously, offer the advanced features of CNCs (Chu et al., 2020). Overwhelmingly, F-CNCs are constructed in a post-treatment manner, which relies on the covalent bonding or physical interactions between fluorophores and CNC scaffold (Ding et al., 2017). The specific construction method depends on both the chemical structure of fluorophore and the type of groups carried by CNCs (Li, Liu, et al., 2022). Due to their superior properties, such as hydrophilicity, excellent biocompatibility, and nanoscale size, F-CNCs have been an ideal platform for constructions of sensing or imaging systems in environmental and biomedical applications (Dong & Roman, 2007; Liu et al., 2021).

Encouraged by the high sensitivity and simplicity of the fluorescent sensing strategy, a variety of F-CNCs were exploited as fluorescent sensors for evaluation of the environmental contamination degree in the past (Chen et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2017). Fluorescence of the emitters such as gold nanoclusters (Cao et al., 2013), graphene quantum dots (Suryawanshi et al., 2014), or organic dyes (Ye et al., 2020) was adjustable in the presence of targets, achieving the detections of heavy metals. More commonly, non-fluorescent CNC-based composites were innovated to be adsorbents for removing the pollutants by harnessing their extraordinary adsorption capacity. To name a few, one-dimensional CNC-Fe3O4 for capturing Pb2+ (Lu et al., 2016); two-dimensional CNC-based membrane for removing cations (Karim et al., 2016); or three-dimensional hydrogels/aerogels with porous structure (Liu et al., 2019). Regrettably, binding sites for hazards adsorption cannot be well maintained upon the traditional fluorescent functionalization of CNCs, giving rise to the poor adsorption capacity of F-CNCs. Therefore, alternative fluorescent functionalization strategy for simultaneously introducing the fluorescent signal transduction moiety and providing highly dense hazards binding sites on CNCs is of great significance for synchronous target sensing and removal.

The other vital attributes of F-CNCs such as ignorable inflammatory response and cell membrane penetration capability make them competitive candidates for bioimaging applications (Guo et al., 2017; Mahmoud et al., 2010). In the fluorescent probes, hydrophilic CNCs, on one hand, could provide the biocompatibility of nonpolar fluorophores, and on the other hand, temporal resolution was improved by suppressing the diffusions of fluorophores (Fan et al., 2019). Despite great progress has been made, the F-CNCs were served as “always-on” type probes for labeling bacteria or eukaryotic cells (Grate et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2015). They were disabled in showing the changes of subcellular structures or the amount of intracellular substances. In contrast, signal of a fluorescent sensor is activable upon the occurrence of the sensing event (Chen et al., 2019). However, to construct a fluorescent sensor based on F-CNCs has not been attempted since the concerns originated from two aspects: 1) signal transduction efficiency might be quite low due to the poor fluorescent property of F-CNCs; and 2) dispersity of F-CNCs could be negatively influenced in the fluorescent functionalization procedure, impeding the assembly of target-binding receptors.

Inspired by the extensive utilization of citric acid (CA) in not only extraction of CNCs from bioresources (Spinella et al., 2016), but also the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) (Ehrat et al., 2017), we propose a versatile fluorescent CNC-CDs architecture prepared via a hydrolysis‑carbonization strategy. The CNCs are extracted from wood pulp through hydrolysis with citric acid (CA). The as-prepared CNCs were densely modified by CA due to the concurrent esterification reaction. Further, CA molecules are partially carbonized to form blue-emissive CDs on CNCs, forming the CNC-CDs architecture. The morphological, optical, and chemical properties of CA-modified CNCs (CNC-CA) and CNC-CDs are well characterized. Due to the high Hg2+ complexing affinity of the architecture, a field-deployable film for Hg2+ detection and removal is prepared. The Hg2+ dose-dependent fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ removal efficiency, as well as the reusability of the film, are examined for evaluating its application prospect in environmental remediation. Biomedical application of the architecture is exemplified by the construction of a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) sensor for intracellular Fe3+ imaging. In the FRET sensor, the fluorescence of CDs can be effectively quenched by m-tetrakis(4-sulfonatophenyl)porphine (TSPP) due to the efficient FRET from CDs (donor) to TSPP (acceptor). After validation of the Fe3+-dependent FRET tuning and the ratiometric fluorescent response, the sensor is challenged to semi-quantitatively image Fe3+ in living cells.

By advancing an unconventional hydrolysis‑carbonization strategy, CNC-CDs architecture with excellent versatility is prepared. A film comprised of the CNC-CDs is applicable for synchronous Hg2+ detection and removal. Furthermore, semi-quantitative Fe3+ imaging in cytoplasm can be achieved with a FRET sensor based on the CNC-CDs architecture.

Bleached eucalyptus kraft pulp was commercially obtained from Aracruz Cellulose, (Brazil). The pulp had a fiber length of ca. 2 mm, fiber width of ca. 30 μm, degree of polymerization of 1028. The contents for glucan, xylan, and lignin were 77.7 %, 16.4 %, and 0.2 %, respectively. CA (99.5 %) was supplied by J&K Chemical (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine (EDA, 99.5 %), polyethyleneimine (PEI, MW: 70 kDa) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). TSPP (99.5 %) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. FeCl2·4H2O (99.95 %), FeCl3 6H2O (99 %), and Hg(NO3)2·H2O (99.99 %) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Pure water was obtained with a Pall Cascade AN system.

The CNCs were firstly prepared by hydrolyzing wood pulp with CA (Ji et al., 2019). Typically, 1.2 g of bleached wood pulp was added into 50 mL of CA solution (80 wt%), and hydrolyzed at 110 °C for 8 h. Subsequently, 200 mL of pure water was added to the mixture, which was further treated by microwave at a power of 600 W for 10 min to synthesize CDs on the surface of as-prepared CNCs. Varying amounts of EDA was added prior to the carbonization for improving the quantum yield of CDs. Finally, the CNC-CDs architecture was purified by filtrating with a 0.22 μm membrane and mono-dispersed in 100 mL of pure water by sonication at a frequency of 20 kHz for 2 h. The process schematic can be found in Fig. 1 .

The film was prepared by placing 100 mL of the CNC-CDs dispersion (0.2 wt%) in a suction funnel. The dispersion was filtrated against a 0.22 μm membrane under pressure of −0.1 MPa. After the water was completely removed, the film was freeze-dried at −75 °C for 2 h and stored at ambient conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg2+ detection and removal with CNC-CDs film
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescent film (200 mg, 4 cm in diameter) was placed in a Buchner funnel. Afterwards, 50 mL of Hg2+ solutions (0–60 μM) were filtrated through the films. The filtration was finished within 60 min, followed by drying the films in an oven at 50 °C for 1 h. The fluorescence spectra of films were taken to investigate the fluorescent responsiveness of the films. Selectivity test of the film was performed by changing different metal ions (600 μM) in the solution, followed by the drying of film and fluorescence measurements. The Hg2+ removal efficiency ( E ) was calculated using the following equation:
E =
C 0
−
C e
C 0
× 100 %
where C
0 and C
e were initial and equilibrium concentrations of Hg2+, which were measured by inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), respectively. Anti-interference capability of the film in Hg2+ detection and removal was evaluated under the coexistence of varying metal ions.

The FRET sensor was constructed via a layer-by-layer (LBL) self-assembly approach. Certain amounts of cationic PEI (mass ratio of PEI to CNC-CDs: 0–2.5) were added into the CNC-CDs dispersions (25 mL, 0.2 wt%) and ultrasonicated for 30 min. Afterwards, 1 mL of TPPS solution was dropwise added into 25 mL of CNC-CDs@PEI dispersion. The final concentration of TSPP ranged from 0 to 0.8 μM. The FRET sensor with a CNC-CDs@PEI@TSPP configuration was collected by centrifugation at 15,000 rpm for 15 min, and re-dispersed in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) with a concentration of 100 μg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ detection in solution and intracellular Fe3+ imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ solutions (100 μL) with varying concentrations (0–8 μM) were added into 900 μL of CNC-CDs@PEI@TSPP dispersions (100 μg/mL). After standing for 20 min, fluorescent spectra of the mixtures were taken. In the Fe3+ detection experiments, a fixed amount of Fe2+ (1 μM) was coexisted with the Fe3+ and played the role as catalyst for Fe3+-TSPP formation (Huszank et al., 2007). All of the Fe3+ detection experiments were performed in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4), which was degassed with N2 for preventing the oxidation of Fe2+. Specificity test was performed by exposing the sensor to a variety of metal ions under conditions identical to that of Fe3+ detection. Influence of Hg2+ on the FRET sensor was investigated by detecting Fe3+ in the presence of 0.5 μM of Hg2+.

Intracellular Fe3+ imaging with the FRET sensor was performed in HeLa cells. Cytotoxicity of the sensor was investigated by the MTT method. Cellular uptake kinetics of the FRET sensor was investigated by incubating the cells with 10 μg/mL of the sensor for varying time. Intracellular Fe3+ imaging was performed by spiking varying concentrations of Fe3+ (in the coexistence of 1 μM of Fe2+) into the culture medium of HeLa cells pretreated with 10 μg/mL of the FRET sensor for 3 h. The fluorescence images of HeLa cells were taken after incubation for 4 h and three times of washing. The emission intensity, as well as the intensity ratio of the intracellular sensor was analyzed with ImageJ software and compared among the different amounts of Fe3+ spiked cells.

In the composition analysis of cellulosic materials, concentrations of glucose and xylose in the hydrolysate were measured on an HPLC (Agilent, USA). The degree of polymerization measurement was performed by dissolving the samples in cupriethylenediamine and acquiring the viscosity of the solutions by use of a capillary viscometer (150 T447-T450). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired on a JEM-1400 instrument (JEOL, Japan). The Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were performed on a Zetasizer Nano-ZS particle Instrument (Malvern, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CNCs, CNC-CA, and CNC-CDs were acquired with an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) at a wide-angle (5–50°) scan at the rate of 10°/min. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) characterization was recorded on a Vertex 80 V FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, German) by the use of KBr pellets containing samples. All the fluorescence spectra were recorded on an FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm and slits of 2.5 nm. A JSM-7600F scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Japan) equipped with Xray Energy Dispersive Spectroscope (EDS) was applied to perform the morphological investigation, as well as the elemental analysis. The surface area and pore size of CNC-CDs films were recorded on a specific surface area and porosity analyzer (Quantachrome, USA). The concentrations of Hg2+ were measured by an iCAP RQ ICP-MS (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fluorescence images of HeLa cells were gained from an LSM710 laser confocal microscope (Zeiss, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of CNC-CDs architecture via successive hydrolysis‑carbonization
ce:section.ce:para: In the successive hydrolysis‑carbonization process, the amorphous region of cellulose is removed. Simultaneously, C6 hydroxyl groups in the crystalline region esterify with CA, producing CNC-CA nanostructure. The carbonization involves the dehydration of CA on CNCs, forming the blue-emissive CNC-CDs architecture. The chemical, morphological, and optical properties of the products were characterized in detail. For comparing the chemical parameters, hydroxyl-terminated CNCs were parallelly prepared via hydrolysis of the pulp with 10 wt% of HCl (Fig. S1). As summarized in Table 1 , in comparison with CNCs obtained by HCl hydrolysis, CNC-CA reveals higher xylan and lignin residues due to the cancellation of NaOH-treatment process. The carbonization process produces negligible influence on the composition of cellulose, implying that CA rather than cellulose is involved in the formation of CDs. For the degree of polymerization, CNC-CA is slightly lower than CNCs, which is caused by the extensive esterification of cellulose and CA. However, in-situ formation of CDs via carbonization is considered to result in the inter-connections of depolymerized cellulose, as reflected by the increased value (239) of polymerization degree. Quantitatively, the carboxyl content is as high as 1.02 mmol/g for CNC-CA, while the content is 0.89 mmol/g for CNC-CDs. Following the reported method (Spinella et al., 2016), the estimated degree of substitution is 0.20 for CNC-CA, and CDs upload rate is 35.4 mg/g for CNC-CDs (Fig. S2). Noticeably, approximately 87 % of the carboxyl groups are maintained on CNCs after carbonization, which is crucial for efficient heavy metal ion removal and the construction of FRET sensor via electrostatic interaction.

The FT-IR spectrum shows that CNC-CA exhibits a new peak attributed to the stretching vibration of carboxyl groups (1728 cm−1) in comparison to the hydroxyl-terminated CNCs (Fig. 2A). The emergence of CN stretching at 1106 cm−1 can prove the successful in-situ synthesis of N-doped CDs. The XPS results also confirm the successful decoration of CA, and further in-situ formation of CDs (Fig. S3). The TEM images show that both CNC-CA and CNC-CDs possess excellent mono-dispersity (Fig. 2B), ascribing to the electrostatic repulsion among the negatively-charged crystals (Fig. 2C). For CNC-CA, the longitudinal and transverse dimensions are measured as 197.4 ± 35.6 nm and 19.3 ± 3.5 nm, respectively, while the dimensions are 206.4 ± 32.9 and 25.2 ± 4.7 nm for CNC-CDs. Both exhibit good consistency with the DLS measurements (Fig. S4). The in-situ synthesized CDs, which possess a crystalline lattice space of 0.22 nm, are observed on the entire surface of CNCs after the carbonization. According to the XRD diffraction patterns (Fig. 2D) and relevant calculations (Yang et al., 2020), the crystallinity index is 71.43 % for CNC-CDs, extremely close to those of CNC-CA (72.21 %) and hydroxyl-terminated CNCs (72.58 %). The thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermo-gravimetry (DTG) curves show that the thermal stability of CNCs is negligibly influenced (Fig. S5). These results indicate that the successive hydrolysis‑carbonization procedure is actually to introduce and partially dehydrate CA on surface of CNCs. The intrinsic crystallinity of cellulose is not changed, enabling their applications under varying conditions.

The formation of CDs renders non-fluorescent CNCs with bright blue fluorescence (Fig. 2E). Upon the addition of an optimal amount of EDA (45 mmol), the architecture shows an emission peak at 460 nm and a quantum yield of 45 % (Table S1). On the absorption spectrum of CNC-CDs, the n-π* transitions around 350 nm at the edge of the carbon lattice are observed (Fig. 2F) (Fu et al., 2022), also suggesting the successful construction of CNC-CDs architecture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Facile fabrication of the CNC-CDs film
ce:section.ce:para: Portable devices reveal the obvious advantages in deployable target detections and facile removal of hazardous substances. Carboxylated CDs can specifically chelate Hg2+ and produce measurable fluorescent changes (Cui et al., 2015). Therefore, the CNC-CDs architecture was filtrated into the fluorescent film for detecting and removing Hg2+ (Fig. 3A). The un-esterified hydroxyl groups are supposed to be responsible for stabilizing the compact film structure by forming hydrogen bonds among the architectures. The transparent film shows bright blue fluorescence under excitation (365 nm). The morphology of CNC-CDs film was investigated by SEM. For revealing the influences of CDs on the film structure, the morphology of CNC-CA film was investigated in parallel. Both films possess well-distributed and dense mesopores (Fig. 3B). Quantitatively, the specific surface area reaches up to 14.05 m2/g, while the average diameter of mesopores is 3.02 nm for the CNC-CDs film (Table S2). Evidently, the pores on the CNC-CDs film are smaller, which could be attributed to the blocking effect of CD particles. The mesoporous structure of the film can contribute to the improvement of its specific surface area, further promoting the Hg2+ adsorption capacity. The results show that the CNC-CDs film is a promising Hg2+ sensor and adsorbent with abundant target chelation sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive detection and efficient removal of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The detection and removal of Hg2+ were performed by filtrating Hg2+ solution through the CNC-CDs film, as shown in Fig. 4A. Due to the high chelation affinity of the carboxyl groups, Hg2+ can be captured and leads to the fluorescence quenching of CDs by the mechanism of photo-induced electron transfer (PET). For CDs, the LUMO and HOMO orbitals are −4.46 eV and −7.64 eV (Fig. S6), respectively, allowing the electron transfer from LUMO orbital of CDs to d-orbital of Hg2+ (−5.25 eV) (He et al., 2018). By changing the conditions for Hg2+ detection and removal, it was found that pH (3.0–11.0) and temperature (25–75 °C) reveal no impact on the fluorescence quenching of the film and the decrement of Hg2+ in the solution (Fig. S7). The result demonstrates that the film is suitable for extensive applications in harsh environmental conditions. The Hg2+ concentration-dependent fluorescent responsiveness of the film is displayed in Fig. 4B. Fluorescence quenching is produced in the presence of as low as 0.1 μM of Hg2+. Further increased amounts of Hg2+ can cause the thorough switching off of the fluorescence. The relative intensity value (I/I 0) shows a sharply linear decline in the Hg2+ concentration ranges from 0 to 250 nM, in which dynamic Hg2+ detection can be achieved. Following the 3σ criteria, the obtained limit of detection (LOD) is 12.8 nM, which is comparable to those of the most sensitive fluorescent sensors (Table S3). High specificity of the fluorescent film is an essential requirement for Hg2+ detection in environmental samples. As shown in Fig. 4C, obviously, only the presence of Hg2+ quenches the fluorescence of the film. No intensity changes are observed for other metal ions. Besides, responsiveness, as well as the Hg2+ retention capability, of the film is tolerant of the coexistence of other metal ions (Fig. S8), revealing satisfying anti-interference capability. The outstanding specificity of the film is considered to be benefited from the higher binding affinity between carboxyl groups with Hg2+ among the ions (Yu et al., 2019).

The Hg2+ removal efficiency of the CNC-CDs film was tested by monitoring the concentration changes of the Hg2+ solutions. As shown in Fig. 4D, the values of E are larger than 97.33 % while the initial Hg2+ concentration is lower than 35 μM, demonstrating that virtually all Hg2+ is retained. For higher Hg2+ concentrations, reasonable retention rates are achieved as well. The EDS mapping images can further verify the retention of Hg2+ by the film. On the film after the filtration of Hg2+, distribution of mercury is observed (Fig. S9), which accounts for 1.9 % of total elemental content (Table S4). Reusability is another key issue related to the application cost. In the recycling experiment, the CNC-CDs film was renewed by the treatment with EDTA (600 μmol/L), followed by the additional rounds of Hg2+ removal tests. A slight loss in the adsorption efficiency is observed for the film after three cycles (Fig. 4E). The values are 96.17 %, 94.5 %, and 92.27 % for the second, third, and fourth times of Hg2+ (35 μM) filtrations, respectively. Benefitting from the nontrivial optical stability of CDs, repetitious Hg2+ detection is permitted by showing reversible fluorescent changes. Together the fabrication easiness, cost-effectiveness, sensitive detection, and efficient removal of Hg2+ have made the CNC-CDs film possible for large-scale water treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and construction of the CNC-CDs based FRET sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The FRET sensors, which show binary-emissive characteristics upon emissive acceptors are adopted, are capable of excluding the interferences by their inherent ratiometric fluorescence and improving the sensitivity by intensifying the signal changes. The non-trivial biocompatibility of CNC-CDs architecture encouraged us to explore its possibility in the construction of an intracellular FRET sensor. Iron is the most abundant transition metal in human body (Han et al., 2016). Either deficiencies or excesses of Fe3+ can induce physiological disorders. Quantitative or semi-quantitative Fe3+ analysis in biological systems has thus attracted significant attention. Metalation of TSPP by Fe3+ can give rise to its variations in absorbance and fluorescence, making it an ideal acceptor in a FRET system. Therefore, we advance a FRET sensor via the electrostatic-driven LBL self-assemblies of PEI and TSPP on the CNC-CDs architecture (Fig. 5A). The CDs are served as the FRET donor, PEI plays the role of spacer, and TSPP is the FRET acceptor. As shown in Fig. 5B, upon the assembling of PEI, potential of the architecture increases gradually with the increased PEI amount. The conversion of surface charge occurs at a PEI-to-architecture ratio of 1.5, which is considered the optimal PEI amount to completely coat the CNC-CDs, however, with the smallest thickness. The thin-layer PEI spacing is pivotal for efficient FRET from CDs to TSPP. A re-decrease in Zeta potential is observed with increased negatively-charged TSPP concentration, exhibiting a dynamic range of 0–0.6 μM (Fig. 5C). The blue emission of CDs is gradually quenched by TSPP (Fig. 5D), ascribing to the increased FRET efficiency between CDs and TSPP (Fig. S10). In contrast, the red emission of TSPP arises sharply. At a TSPP concentration of 0.6 μM, the Zeta potential, emission intensities of CDs and TSPP, and accordingly, the relative blue-to-red intensity ratio ( I
B/ I
R) get equilibrium (Fig. 5E), indicating the saturated TSPP assembly. The TEM images show that both CNC-CDs@PEI and CNC-CDs@PEI@TSPP have excellent mono-dispersity, regardless of the coatings (Fig. S11). In the FT-IR spectrum, the deformation vibration band of benzene (810 cm−1), which belongs to the aromatic TSPP appears in the spectrum of CNC-CDs@PEI@TSPP, confirming the successful construction of the FRET sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Fe3+ by tuning the FRET efficiency and fluorescence ratio of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
For the FRET sensor, detection of Fe3+ is realized by the mechanism shown in Fig. 6A. Due to the consistent donor-acceptor distance, the FRET efficiency relies only on the resonance effect between the FRET pair, which is determined by the spectral overlap between the CDs emission and the TSPP absorption. As shown in Fig. 6B, the absorption bands at 413 nm and 434 nm are gradually reduced, and oppositely, the band at 393 nm is enhanced upon TSPP chelating with increased amounts of Fe3+. The spectral transition from TSPP to Fe3+-TSPP induces the ineffectiveness of their overlap with the emission profile of CDs, giving rise to the suppressed FRET efficiency. Consequently, the sensor exhibits a ratiometric fluorescent response to Fe3+: the blue emission of CDs is gradually recovered, whereas the red emission of TSPP is quenched since the perturbation of energy levels of the delocalized π and π* frontier orbitals by the central Fe3+ (Fig. 6C) (Valicsek & Horváth, 2013). Relying on the ratiometric fluorescent response, visualized detection of Fe3+ can be achieved relying on the continuous color change from magenta to blue. The value of I
B/ I
R is closely related to the Fe3+ concentration (Fig. 6D). The dynamic range is determined to be 0–40 nM, and the LOD is 6.9 nM. The Fe3+ detection sensitivity achieved by this FRET sensor ranks top among reported FRET-based Fe3+ sensors (Table S5), owing to the superior FRET efficiency, avid TSPP/Fe3+ complexing, and more importantly, dramatic signal response by oppositely tuned dual fluorescence.

Specificity of the sensor is another key issue to be concerned since various metal ions coexist with Fe3+ in biological matrix. The specificity for Fe3+ is investigated by exposing the FRET sensor to various metal ions such as abundant physiological cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+), and trace cations (Fe2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cr3+). As shown in Fig. 6E no obvious changes in I
B/ I
R are observed for most of the metal ions, demonstrating the satisfying specificity of the FRET sensor. The exceptions are observed for Fe2+, Zn2+, and Hg2+ since they can be complexed by TSPP and lead to the fluorescence quenching of TSPP to certain extents. Fortunately, the slight change of their absorption bands negligibly affects the FRET efficiency of the sensor (Fig. S12). Therefore, fluorescence recovery of CDs is unavailable for these metal ions. Moreover, fluorescence quenching of CNC-CDs by Hg2+ is shielded due to the coating of cationic PEI. Therefore, the FRET sensor exhibits similar responsiveness to Fe3+ upon the coexistence of low-abundant Hg2+ (0.5 μM) (Fig. S13). The results clearly show that the FRET sensor, in comparison to the sole TSPP probe, is advantageous in terms of specificity by outputting the ratiometric fluorescent signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization and semi-quantification of intracellular Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: To visualize and semi-quantitatively evaluate the intracellular Fe3+ is of particular importance for the nosogenesis of Fe3+-dependent diseases and clinical diagnosis. The FRET sensor was challenged to visualize Fe3+ in HeLa cells. The MTT assay reveals that the FRET sensor has a negligible effect on cell viability in 10 h, being featured with low cytotoxicity (Fig. S14). Cellular uptake of the sensor was investigated by time-dependent fluorescence imaging. By recording the fluorescence in cytoplasm, the FRET sensor is found to heavily enter the cells, and get equilibrium at 2.5 h (Fig. S15).

Fig. 7 shows the microscopic fluorescent images of native and varying concentrations of Fe3+-treated HeLa cells with the FRET sensor. The native cells exhibited a blue-to-red fluorescence intensity ratio ( I
B/ I
R) of 0.24:1, identical to the FRET sensor in solution. For the Fe3+-treated cells, remarkable enhancement of blue emission and obvious quenching of red emission are observed. The I
B/ I
R depends on the amount of Fe3+ added to the culture media. Quantitatively, the vales are 0.54:1, 1.04:1, 1.65:1, 4.03:1, and 16.19:1 for 25, 50, 250, 500, and 800 nM of Fe3+ treated cell, respectively. As a result, the merged cell images display readable color changes along with the gradually increased Fe3+ concentration. Conceivably, the cell-color change achieved by the FRET sensor offers the feasibility of semi-quantitatively indicating the amount of intracellular Fe3+.

By taking advantage of organic acid in terms of hydrolyzing cellulose and forming CDs, an unconventional preparation strategy for fluorescently-functionalized CNCs is exploited. The CNC-CDs architecture is featured with superior optical and chemical properties, rendering them versatile in the constructions of novel sensing/removal/imaging platforms. Relying on the Hg2+-responsiveness of the CDs, the architecture-based fluorescent film is successfully used for the detection of Hg2+ with high sensitivity and synchronous Hg2+ removal with high efficiency. The biomedical application of the architecture is exemplified by the construction of a FRET sensor for Fe3+ in living cells. Metalation of TSPP by Fe3+ suppresses the FRET efficiency, causing the ratiometric fluorescent response of the FRET sensor. Benefited from this, semi-quantitative Fe3+ imaging in cytoplasm has been achieved. Advancing the one-pot synthesis of CNC-CDs in a successive hydrolysis‑carbonization procedure represents a new trend in functionalization of cellulose nanocrystals/nanofibers, which can find diverse applications in areas of, but not limited to, environment and biomedicine.

Ruyi Li: investigation, data curation, formal analysis, visualization. Fangyuan Liang: investigation, data curation, formal analysis, visualization. Xianyun Hu: resource collection, and funding acquisition. Huiyang Bian: methodology. Chao Deng: formal analysis and validation. Farzad Seidi: methodology. Baiyang Zhang: investigation and formal analysis. Huining Xiao: supervision, project administration, and writing - review & editing. Yuqian Liu: conceptualization, methodology, funding acquisition, writing - original draft, and writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20210630); Natural Science Foundation of China (81860317); Science and Technology Department of Guizhou Province (20192821); and NSERC Canada.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2022.120073.
